text: Robotic lumbar hernia repair with mesh. Traditionally, due to the location of lumbar hernias, repair occurs via an open technique. In this video, we will discuss a robotic preperitoneal technique for repair of a lumbar hernia. The patient is a seventy-eight year old female who presented to clinic with a seven to eight months of right lower back bulge and pain. Of note, she had suffered from these symptoms before. In two thousand seven, she had had her right lumbar hernia repaired via an open technique with mesh. One year later, in two thousand eight, she suffered recurrence and again had an open repair of her lumbar hernia with mesh.

As a part of the diagnostic workup, we obtained a CT abdomen pelvis which showed a right lumbar hernia containing retroperitoneal and perineptic fat. After discussion and case conference, we decided the best operation for this patient would be a robotic preperitoneal hernia repair with mesh and a fibrin seal.

The patient is positioned in left lateral decubitus, and the abdomen is accessed with a varus needle for insufflation. Two additional ports are placed in the right abdomen under direct visualization. The da Vinci robot is docked, and the camera is inserted through an 8mm mid-abdominal port. The surgeon begins retroperitoneal dissection using blunt and sharp techniques along with electrocautery, entering the peritoneum near the liver. The hernia sac and retroperitoneal fat are identified, with previous mesh visible inferiorly and posteriorly. The surgeon dissects around the hernia sac, removes the previous mesh, and fully reduces the hernia sac contents into the abdomen.

The surgeon closes the hernia defect with a running O stratific suture, reinforced with interrupted PDS sutures. The defect is measured, and a twelve by twelve piece of mesh with rounded corners is aligned to cover the defect. The superior lip of the mesh is fixated using a running stratifix suture. The excised mesh is removed through a port site using an endo-catch bag, and fascial stitches are placed for later closure.

The mesh is visibly fixated on the superior edge, overlying the closed hernia defect. Fiber and glue are applied to the mesh to secure its fixation to the abdominal wall. The flap is closed using a running O stratifix suture.
The surgeon inspects the abdomen to ensure hemostasis, removes the laparoscopic ports, and closes the fascia, marking the end of the procedure.

The purpose of this video is to present a minimally invasive approach to recurrent lumbar hernia repair, specifically a robotic preperitoneal approach, as a promising alternative to open repair of lumbar hernias.
